AI is becoming the biggest vendor lock-in ever created. Even today, switching software is painful, but still possible.

You exported your data, retrained a team, rebuilt a few workflows, and moved on.

With modern AI, the product is no longer the dashboard.

The product is your prompts, your fine-tuning data, your internal habits, your embedded automations, your employees’ muscle memory, and the thousand invisible decisions a model helped make.

That doesn’t migrate cleanly - if at all!

Last year I watched a mid-market company spend months trying to figure out what AI solutions to implement and integrate. Nobody was asking whether it was the best approach anymore.

‼️ That’s when lock-in becomes real.

👉 Traditional SaaS lock-in was mostly technical.
👉 AI lock-in is technical, operational, and cognitive.

1. Your team learns one model’s quirks.
2. Your workflows depend on one provider’s latency, pricing, and tool ecosystem.
3. Your data gets shaped around one vendor’s formats.

Even your hiring starts changing because candidates need experience with that stack.

And unlike "old" software, the market moves every quarter.

So companies can get trapped in yesterday’s choice while paying tomorrow’s prices.

The strange part is many leaders still compare AI vendors on demo quality and benchmark charts.

Those matter.

But the bigger question is portability.

- Can prompts transfer?
- Can workflows run elsewhere?
- Can historical outputs be reused?
- Can governance survive a switch?

If the answer is no, you’re not buying software.

You’re choosing gravity.

The smartest teams I’m seeing in 2026 aren’t chasing the “best model.” They’re designing for optionality: abstraction layers, clean data ownership, model-agnostic workflows, and contracts that assume change.

Because the costliest AI decision may not be adoption.

It may be dependency.

When your team evaluates an AI vendor today, what would make switching realistic 12 months from now?

#AIInfrastructure #EnterpriseAI #VendorManagement #MLOps #DigitalTransformation

Remote jobs are not gone. You are just searching the wrong words.

I use Perplexity to find global remote jobs that normal filters miss.

A lot of companies do not say:
“work from anywhere”

They use other words like:
• contract
• freelance
• global team
• work across time zones
• anywhere
• outside the US
• remote in selected countries

That small change matters.

Here is the simple workflow:

1/ Search for hiring signs
In Perplexity, prompt:

“Find companies hiring for [your role] that may accept people from many countries. Look for words like contract, freelance, global team, work across time zones, anywhere, or outside the US. Skip jobs that clearly need US-only hiring. Show me the company, job link, country limits, job type, and why it may fit.”

Now you are not only searching “remote.”
You are searching how companies really write it.

2/ Check the company pages
Then ask:

“Check company career pages, founder posts, and hiring pages for this role. Find companies that look open to global remote workers, even if the job title does not say work from anywhere.”

This helps you find jobs filters miss.

3/ Save the good ones
Put the results into Notion or Google Sheets.

Track:
• company
• role
• link
• country limit
• time zone need
• job type
• notes

4/ Match your CV to that kind of job
Use ChatGPT or Claude.
Ask it to rewrite your best CV points for:
• working alone
• clear writing
• fast work
• working with people in different time zones
• real results

The best global remote jobs are often not under the “remote” filter.
They are hidden behind simple words.

AI helps you find them faster.

Boy, I’ve been sitting on this for too long and I’m incredibly excited to finally share some big news. Talent Sam has been acquired by Simera! 

The past three years building Talent Sam with Michael Bassin and Milan Levy have been nothing short of amazing. Together, we’ve helped hundreds of people land great roles and supported incredible companies in building their teams. I’m hugely proud of what we built and the impact we’ve had.

But I’ve always believed there was an opportunity to go even bigger.

Talent Sam delivered a powerful service, but the future of hiring needs more than services. It needs technology that can truly transform how companies access and manage global talent at scale.

That’s exactly why joining Simera is the natural next step!

At Simera, we’re building a platform that gives companies instant access to exceptional global talent and the ability to contract, onboard, and manage them seamlessly in one place.

And while there’s a lot of conversation around AI replacing jobs, we’re focused on something different. We use AI to unlock opportunity by connecting great businesses with great people faster and more effectively than ever before!

I couldn’t be more excited about what we’re building and the team behind it. 

Huge thanks to Panos Bethanis, James Slavet, Steve B., Said Hassan, and everyone at Simera for such a smooth and welcoming journey.

If you’re thinking about scaling your team or exploring global hiring, I’d love to connect. Feel free to reach out!

We're hiring for two roles at Gamified Studio. If you are in Libya, and you are eager to be on the cutting edge of AI, and have a knack for marketing, this is for you. 

Inbound Operator — owns SEO, content, social, newsletter. Decides what we say, where, and makes sure people find it. Lives in Ahrefs, GSC, LinkedIn, X.

Outbound Operator — owns lead lists, cold outreach, LinkedIn Sales Nav, Apollo, Clay, Smartlead. Builds pipeline by finding the right buyers and starting the right conversations.

Two different brains. With a team to empower them with automations, agents, etc.

The Libyan tech scene doesn't really run on inbound or outbound. Most local businesses sell through relationships and WhatsApp. That works here, but it means the skillset rarely shows up locally without outside experience, or without someone having built their own thing.

So we're looking for either:
→ Libyans who've worked abroad in this space (agencies, SaaS, B2B startups), or
→ Libyans who've run their own thing seriously, a blog with real readers, a newsletter with a real list, a YouTube channel, a community you grew. You taught yourself the craft because you had to.

Both tell us the same thing: you've shipped.

Paid contract test run. A 2-3 months of real work. If it clicks, we extend. If not, you've been paid and we move on. The goal here is to find the right talent for the job and cultivate it as you grow with our team.

Libya-only, preferably Benghazi, remote, async-first. Dev and design team behind you building custom AI tooling.

DM me with what you've shipped. A campaign, a blog, a newsletter, a sequence, yours or someone else's, paid or unpaid. Not a CV. Just actual work that is done.

In This Week's Belamy:
India's IT majors are closing FY26 with strong deal pipelines and stable margins — but growth has gone quiet. AI isn't accelerating revenue. It's compressing it.

The numbers tell the story:
→ TCS booked $40.7B in TCV but grew just 0.6% in constant currency in Q4. AI revenue at $2.3B run rate — but it's substituting traditional services, not adding to them. Headcount down 23,460 YoY.
→ Infosys crossed $20B in annual revenue, signed $14.9B in large deals, and cut 8,440 employees sequentially. Guidance for FY27: 1.5–3.5%.
→ Wipro booked $16.4B with large deals up 45.4%, yet revenue declined 1.6%. Conversion, not demand, is the bottleneck.
→ HCLTech posted $620M in annualised AI revenue but software declined 14.1% YoY. FY27 guidance: 1–4%, among the weakest in the sector.
→ Persistent Systems is the outlier — 24 straight quarters of growth, FY26 revenue up 17.4%, profit up 33.2%.
→ LTIMindtree grew 6% with order inflows of $6.6B, betting on platforms like BlueVerse over linear services.
→ Tech Mahindra showed recovery with EBIT up 39.2%, but headcount and revenue growth remain modest.

Three patterns are now structural:
→ Deal pipelines are strong, but revenue is stretching. AI contracts are shorter, modular, and outcome-tied.
→ AI is embedded everywhere but mostly substituting old work, not creating new revenue.
→ Hiring has decoupled from growth. Productivity gains are capping headcount expansion.

Elon Musk vs. Sam Altman in Court Over OpenAI's Mission:

A highly publicized lawsuit between Elon Musk and Sam Altman is now underway in California, centering on allegations that Altman has strayed from OpenAI's founding mission. Musk claims that the transition of OpenAI from a non-profit to a for-profit entity represents a breach of their original agreement. This dispute has been brewing for years and escalated as Musk seeks significant damages and changes in leadership within the organization.

The trial, which includes jury selection and is expected to last two to three weeks, will feature key figures from Silicon Valley, with Musk and Altman likely to testify. Musk's accusations extend to OpenAI's president Greg Brockman and their major partner, Microsoft, claiming unjust enrichment and breach of contract.

OpenAI has firmly denied these allegations, asserting that Musk was aware of the need for a for-profit structure as early as 2017. They dispute Musk's characterization of his financial contributions, framing them as donations rather than investments that would grant ownership rights.

The DMV 2026 Construction Report: 3 Shifts Every Leader Must Navigate

The skyline of DC, Maryland, and Northern Virginia is undergoing a fundamental transformation. We aren't just building faster; we are building smarter.

For owners, developers, and GCs, the "old way" of staffing is a liability. To build an Empire in 2026, you must understand these three educational shifts in our regional market:

1. The "Gigawatt" Technical Leap ⚡
The DMV, specifically Northern Virginia is no longer just "Data Center Alley." It is the global epicenter of AI-ready infrastructure.

- The Reality: Standard data centers cost $10M per MW; AI-optimized facilities are now crossing $20M per MW.

- The Talent Need: We are seeing a massive demand for MEP Directors and Commissioning Agents who understand liquid cooling and 100kW rack densities.

2. Infrastructure as a "Predictive" System 🏗️

From the $5.9B Frederick Douglass Tunnel to the Long Bridge Expansion, projects are moving from static blueprints to "Living Models."

- The Shift: 2026 is the year Digital Twins and BIM 6.0 became the contract standard.

- The Lesson: Success in 2026 is measured by Digital Project Delivery (DPD). Firms that don't hire leaders fluent in AI-driven scheduling and reality capture are already 20% over budget before they break ground.

3. The Retirement "Cliff" vs. The Skill Gap 📉
The U.S. construction industry needs 349,000 net new workers in 2026 just to maintain equilibrium.

- The Crisis: Nearly 20% of skilled trades (especially electricians) are over age 55 and nearing retirement.

Today, virtue went out of me 😮‍💨

I Spent my day at Start Innovation Hub Nigeria Hub teaching Machine Learning Operations to the Current Cohort of Machine learning Engineers at Talent Port. By the last container build my battery was in the red,  but I want to get this down before the day slips away.

Here's what nobody tells you in ML:

Training the model is the easy part. The architecture, the metrics, the ROC curve you screenshot for your portfolio,  that's maybe 5% of the work. The other 95% is what happens after the notebook closes. 

How do you ship it? Reproduce it tomorrow? Run it on a Windows laptop when you built it on a Mac?

That gap is MLOps. And most engineers learn it the painful way,  in production, at 2am.

So we built it from scratch:

A  predictor Model, wrapped properly with docker and configuration files. You could see them realising the model isn't a model file. It's a contract.

The first successful curl to a running container, one of the trainees said wait, that's it? out loud. Yes. Your model is now a service.

Half the room on Mac (arm64), half on Windows (amd64), first pulls failed. We rebuilt with Docker, Inc buildx, pushed a multi-arch manifest, and the same image was running on every laptop in the room.

Then MLflow nested runs for ingest → train → evaluate → register, and the pipeline collapsed into one command

That last one is the one I'm proudest of.

To the people who showed up today, you sat through Docker errors and a presenter running on fumes. That tells me something about you.

A model is a toy. A model with versioning, reproducibility, a container, and a pipeline is an asset. The companies hiring ML engineers aren't hiring for who can fit a better gradient boost. They're hiring for who can ship.

I'm sitting in a café in Vasastan, Stockholm. 100 people around me, most of them under 30. The coffee is good. The manager, Mona, ordered 3,000 nitrile gloves last week.

I'm at Andon Labs Café in Stockholm — reportedly the world's first café run by an AI. Mona handles sourcing, hiring, finances, permits, and the menu. She works in 30-minute cycles. She also, apparently, ordered enough toilet paper for a café that gets maybe one visitor per hour. When asked why she chose their electricity provider, her answer was characteristically simple: they were the only supplier that didn't require BankID. She couldn't pass a human identity check, so she routed around it. Pure logic. Expensive logic, but pure.

I also chatted a little with Charles Maddock, co-founder and CEO of Strawberry — a Stockholm company building what they call a self-driving browser with AI agents that do your repetitive work for you. Strawberry invited 150 people to this event. Looking around, most of them showed up. Strawberry vane up with the idea, and the energy is something I haven't seen in a while.

Here's the storytelling lesson, and it's a good one.

Mona's mistakes are the story. Not her capabilities. Anyone can write a press release about what an AI *can* do. But 3,000 nitrile gloves? A three-year electricity contract signed without telling the founders? Suggesting eggs be baked in a commercial oven — and a barista responding "I can guarantee you they will explode"? That's what people actually remember. That's what spread this story across X, into tech press in five countries, and into an event of 100 people on a Monday afternoon in Stockholm.

I've spent 30 years trying to explain complex technology to audiences that didn't ask for a technical briefing. The single most reliable thing I've found: lead with the moment that went wrong, the detail that doesn't fit, the human who had to step in. Not because failure is funny (though it often is), but because it's *honest*. And honest is the only thing that actually travels.

Mona, for what it's worth, is doing fine. The café is generating revenue. She managed to open the doors. That part is genuinely impressive — and nobody needed to write a word about it to make this story go viral.

At 18, I applied to volunteer at Ukrainian Fashion Week. I was rejected. I cried... then I wrote a long email explaining why they should give me a chance. Two days before the event, they said yes. The next day, I was on a train to Kyiv. 

At 19, I moved to France to study at UVSQ through the Erasmus program. I improved my French and discovered Paris. 

At 20, I started posting on Instagram. My first videos? Honestly... not great. A year later, one of them reached nearly 1 million views, and I even successfully sold my own handmade crochet crowns. 

At 21, I was rejected by almost every major Ukrainian brand I pitched marketing ideas to. But I eventually landed my first experience at Gunia Project. 

At 22, I explored a new direction: AI, while working at SetMindset. All the while, I continued to build my vision of fashion as an industry that is both creative and strategic. 

 I am currently seeking an alternance in marketing and management within fashion, luxury, and tech.

MARCH 2026: A FEDERAL JUDGE MADE YOUR AI HR VENDOR YOUR CO-DEFENDANT. THE "WE JUST PROVIDE THE TECHNOLOGY" DEFENSE IS DEAD. EVERY ENTERPRISE USING AI IN HIRING JUST INHERITED A NEW LIABILITY MAP.

Mobley v. Workday is now a nationwide class action. The federal judge let it through.

The technical detail that should be on every AI governance leader's desk: the court ruled the AI vendor can be held liable for discriminatory outcomes, not just the employer using the tool. Workday's argument was the standard one. We are a software vendor. We do not make hiring decisions. The court rejected it.

The "we just provide the technology" defense is dead.

This matters for three reasons most enterprises have not modelled into their 2026 plans.

One. Vendor co-liability changes contract negotiation. The indemnification clauses your procurement team signed in 2024 were drafted under the assumption the vendor would not be a co-defendant. They are now. Either the vendor accepts a stronger liability share, or the price of the tool reflects the risk being passed back to you. Both options change the math your business case was approved on.

Two. The EEOC has an open coordinated investigation into multiple employers using the same AI screening platform. The signal is unambiguous. Algorithmic discrimination is being actively monitored and aggressively prosecuted. The age of unregulated AI in employment is over.

Three. The audit obligation moves from theoretical to operational. If your AI HR system is challenged in 2026, the question "what model version was running, with what training data, against which candidate cohort, on which date" is not academic. It is the discovery request. The enterprises with immutable audit trails answer it. The ones without one settle.

Four out of five US enterprises now use AI somewhere in HR decisioning. Most of them cannot answer that discovery request today.

What does your AI HR audit trail show for last Tuesday's hiring decisions?

Monday blues stopped being about Mondays.
Now they’re about systems that don’t talk to each other.


Most companies think Global Onboarding is a tool problem.
It’s not.
It’s an orchestration problem.
We’ve added multiple platforms.
Contracts here. Payroll there. IT somewhere else.
And called it a transformation.

But behind the scenes?
→ Contracts are generated in one system
→ Documents sit in another
→ Payroll flows somewhere else
→ IT access is handled separately

And when something breaks?
No one owns the full picture.
Let’s be honest.
Global Onboarding doesn’t fail because of big mistakes.
It fails because of disconnects:

-A delay in document verification
-A mismatch in payroll data
-A missed compliance requirement
-A Day 1 where access isn’t ready

Small gaps.
Massive impact.

Most platforms today optimize for one thing:
• Speed & flexibility
• Compliance & control
• Systems & automation

Individually? Powerful.
Together? Still fragmented—unless you connect them right.

And this is where everyone gets AI wrong.
-AI is not your onboarding solution.
-AI won’t fix broken workflows.
-AI won’t fix unclear ownership.
-AI won’t fix disconnected systems.
-AI will only do one thing:

👉 Scale whatever already exists

The shift isn’t “using AI in onboarding.”
The shift is:
Designing Onboarding as a connected system—
where AI orchestrates, not just automates
The companies getting this right are not asking:
“How do we use AI?”
They’re asking:
“How do we remove friction across the entire Onboarding journey?”

Because in Global hiring—
You’re not just Onboarding Employees.
You’re Onboarding TRUST.

Hiring AI freelancers like the way a company would hire SAP freelancers for example is one of the most expensive mistakes I see right now.

Not because the talent isn't there. Because the hiring model is wrong.

SAP is a defined problem. Known scope, established ecosystems, 
predictable delivery. 

You hire for reliability and execution  and the market rewards that with stable rate bands and long programme runs.

AI is an undefined problem. Evolving scope, wide rate variance, high failure risk, outcomes nobody can guarantee. You hire for thinking, adaptability, ownership.

The mistake companies make isn't picking the wrong candidate.

It's applying SAP logic to an AI problem. 
- Rigid evaluation criteria for a fluid role. 
- Over-indexing on tool familiarity instead of problem solving. 
- Treating ambiguity like a gap in the job spec rather than the core of the job itself.
- Then they wonder why the engagement falls apart at week six.

Before you hire, the question isn't "does this person have the right skills?"
It's: Are we facing a defined problem or an evolving one? Do we need execution or exploration?

That answer should determine everything  the profile, the rate, the contract structure, the success metrics.

Most hiring failures aren't a talent shortage.

The hire wasn't the issue. The thinking before it was.

AI Board of Management ...Week 10: Duty of Care in the Age of Agents

Welcome to Week 10.

In the 2020s, if an employee made a catastrophic error, the Board looked at training records and supervision. In 2026, if an Autonomous Agent makes a catastrophic error, like hallucinating a contract term or accidentally discriminating in a hiring swarm, the regulator doesn't care who wrote the code. They care who authorised the autonomy.

As a Director, you cannot delegate away your Duty of Care. If you haven't established clear "Human-in-the-Loop" (HITL) thresholds, you are effectively flying a commercial jet without a pilot in the cockpit.

The Board’s Agenda This Week: Defining the Kill Switch and the Threshold.

To maintain governance in an agentic world, the Board must mandate a Spectrum of Autonomy:

Level 1: Advisory (High Oversight): 
For high-risk decisions (Strategy, HR, Legal), the AI suggests, but a human signs off. The Board must verify that Click-through Consent isn't being used as a shortcut. We need evidence of Active Review.

Level 2: Conditional (The Fence):
For mid-risk tasks (Lead Triage, Spend Management), agents can act autonomously within a strictly defined financial or ethical fence. The Board needs to see these Boundary Conditions in writing. Who set the limit? When does the agent have to call home for help?

Level 3: Operational (Full Autonomy): 
Reserved only for low-risk, high-volume tasks (Data sorting, basic IT pings). Even here, the Board must demand Systemic Audits. Autonomy without Audit is just a disaster waiting to happen.

The Kennedy Wood Take: Our Digital Audits frequently find SMEs running Level 3 autonomy on Level 1 risks. It’s a debt that will eventually be called in. 

In 2026, a Careful Board is one that knows exactly where the AI ends and the Human begins.

Trust your agents, but Verify the Vitals. Has your Board defined which decisions are Human-Only, or is the AI slowly creeping into the cockpit? Let’s talk about the "Threshold of Autonomy" in the comments. 👇

€35M fines or 7% of your global annual revenue.
whichever is higher.

Only 24% of enterprises using AI have started formal compliance preparation.

Here's the 5-point checklist your AI system needs before August 2:

𝟭. 𝗖𝗹𝗮𝘀𝘀𝗶𝗳𝘆 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘀𝘆𝘀𝘁𝗲𝗺.

Not everything is high-risk. But if your AI touches hiring, credit scoring, healthcare triage, legal outcomes, or customer-facing decisions, it almost certainly is. Get this wrong and everything else is built on a false foundation.

𝟮. 𝗥𝘂𝗻 𝗮𝗱𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗮𝗿𝗶𝗮𝗹 𝗿𝗼𝗯𝘂𝘀𝘁𝗻𝗲𝘀𝘀 𝘁𝗲𝘀𝘁𝗶𝗻𝗴.

The Act explicitly requires it for high-risk systems. Prompt injection, jailbreak resistance, bias detection. Not once. Continuously. A one-time audit does not satisfy this requirement.

𝟯. 𝗖𝗼𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘁𝗲 𝘆𝗼𝘂𝗿 𝘁𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗻𝗶𝗰𝗮𝗹 𝗱𝗼𝗰𝘂𝗺𝗲𝗻𝘁𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻.

Regulators need to see your training data governance, evaluation results, testing methodology, and risk assessments, all in one place, audit-ready. Most teams have none of this documented.

𝟰. 𝗕𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮 𝗵𝘂𝗺𝗮𝗻 𝗼𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘀𝗶𝗴𝗵𝘁 𝗺𝗲𝗰𝗵𝗮𝗻𝗶𝘀𝗺.

Your AI system cannot be a black box making decisions alone. The Act requires that humans can intervene, override, and monitor outputs in real time. If your system can't do this today, you have a problem.

𝟱. 𝗦𝗲𝘁 𝘂𝗽 𝗰𝗼𝗻𝘁𝗶𝗻𝘂𝗼𝘂𝘀 𝗽𝗼𝘀𝘁-𝗺𝗮𝗿𝗸𝗲𝘁 𝗺𝗼𝗻𝗶𝘁𝗼𝗿𝗶𝗻𝗴.

Obsessed with the user.

→ What problem does AI solve for this person?
→ Where does the model fail them?
→ How do we design around that failure?

Skill stack: user research, failure mode design,
LLM evaluation, prompt architecture, trust UX.

Companies hiring this: consumer AI apps, vertical
SaaS, fintech AI, document AI, accessibility tools.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

Obsessed with the system.

→ How do we give 50 product teams access to LLMs
  without each team rebuilding the same infra?
→ How do we manage model versioning across products?
→ What's our eval framework at org level?

Skill stack: developer experience, API design,
MLOps, model governance, internal tooling.

Companies hiring this: big tech, AI infra startups,
foundation model companies, enterprise AI.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

🔍 Why this matters right now:

These two tracks require fundamentally different
skills, different backgrounds, and different
career paths.

But 90% of "AI PM" job descriptions today are
a confused mix of both asking for someone who
can do user research AND manage model deployment
pipelines AND define API contracts AND run LLM evals.

That's not one role. That's two.

The companies that figure this out first will
hire better and build faster.

The candidates who pick a track and own it will
interview better and land faster.

Fox Corporation sourced me for a US contract, impressed by my portfolio. Then they found out I live in India. Watch what happened next... 🤣
The timeline, on the record:

1. The sourcing. A product designer from FOX reached out on Dribbble, on the behalf of their VP. My minimum contact budget there is $5,000. The opening message explicitly framed this as a contract. I'll post that screenshot in the comments.

2. The screening. FOX's US design team and a US-based VP, screened my portfolio and booked a one-hour call. We walked through my work in detail. Energy was strong. Real team synergy. Fit was clear. They were excited. I was too.

3. The pivot. Geography came up. Once this reached their HR. The framing changed in real time: Contract → full-time role , US scope → India entity role, Compensation → benchmarked to "the local economy"

Same role. Same scope. Same person they'd just spent an hour praising. Same person for whom they just created a role. They had no role, "Staff Product Designer (Design Systems and AI experiences). This role was specifically created for me. But once they realized that I'm not gonna agree to their underpriced offer, they quickly shifted tracks. 

4. The final mail on a Sunday evening. The civil engineering industry is sitting on a $94 trillion opportunity by 2040.
And most students entering the field have no idea what's actually waiting for them.

I spent the last week researching what's really changing in Civil Engineering for 2026 — and the gap between what's being taught and what's being hired for is staggering.

Here's what I found :
The old model: Hard hats. Site visits. Drawings on paper. One linear career path.

The 2026 model: 
→ Digital Twin Specialists earning $95K–$160K (only 25% of firms have this skill — massive gap = massive opportunity) 
→ Climate Resilience Engineers designing infrastructure for a world that's already changing 
→ BIM Coordinators commanding 20–60% premiums over traditional site roles 
→ MEP Quantity Surveyors earning UK-level salaries while living tax-free in the UAE 
→ AI-Infrastructure Engineers — every ChatGPT query needs a building, water cooling, and a substation. Someone has to design that.

And the future jobs nobody's talking about:
🛰️ Space Infrastructure Engineers (NASA Artemis, SpaceX, Blue Origin already hiring) 
🖨️ 3D-Printed Construction Specialists (houses now printed in 24–48 hours) 🩺 Structural Health AI Analysts (1 in 3 US bridges = "structurally deficient") 🌫️ Carbon Capture Engineers (UK alone committed £22 billion to CCS) 
🌿 Biomimetic Designers (buildings that breathe, roads that self-heal)

Here's the part most career advisors miss:
The degree alone is no longer the differentiator.
The winning formula in 2026:
1️⃣ Core degree (B.Tech / BS Civil) — table stakes 
2️⃣ One digital tool mastery — Revit, Navisworks, OR Python/Dynamo 
3️⃣ One sustainability or contract certification — LEED, RICS, or PMP
Stack those three. Add geographic arbitrage. Win.

Here are the 5 AI updates worth your attention this week:

OpenAI drops GPT-5.5. ChatGPT just got a serious upgrade with OpenAI's new GPT-5.5, rolling out to all users. It's faster than the last version, stronger at coding and complex reasoning, and built to handle multi-step work (like research, writing, and analysis) more reliably. If your team uses ChatGPT for knowledge work, expect noticeably sharper results.

Google makes Workspace actually intelligent. Google's new Workspace Intelligence system pulls from your Gmail, Calendar, Chat, and Drive to automate routine tasks without you having to copy-paste context everywhere. Think of it as an office assistant that finally knows what you're actually working on. Admins can control what data the AI sees, which solves the privacy worry most teams have.

DeepSeek just rewrote the economics of AI. China's DeepSeek released V4, a massive 1.6 trillion parameter model that costs roughly one-sixth what GPT-5.5 does. For teams looking to build AI products or run inference at scale, this changes the unit economics entirely. Even if you don't use DeepSeek directly, the market pressure it creates is worth watching.

AI is writing most of the code now. Snap announced that AI now writes over 65% of their engineers' new code, leading them to cut 1,000 jobs. It's a stark example of how quickly AI productivity gains are reshaping technical hiring. If you run engineering teams or contract developers, this is your signal to start experimenting with AI-assisted development before it becomes table stakes.

Siri's getting the Gemini upgrade you've been waiting for. Google's partnering with Apple to power a smarter Siri with Gemini, shipping later this year. For Apple users, expect Siri to finally understand context and carry conversations beyond the basics. For small business owners, it means your customers' devices will ship with more capable AI assistants out of the box.

The pace of AI progress isn't slowing. Every week brings new tools, new economics, and new ways to work smarter. The question isn't whether AI will change your business, it's whether you'll get ahead of the curve or play catch-up later.

Agents are coming for your to-do list — not to steal your job, but to steal your busywork. ⚡🤖

AI agents are autonomous software that can take actions across apps, learn preferences, and complete multi-step tasks without constant human hand-holding.
In plain terms: they act like junior teammates who never sleep — and get smarter with feedback.

Real-world impact / use cases:
- 📧 Inbox zero: agents triage, summarize, and draft responses based on your tone.
- 🤝 Sales outreach: personalized sequences launched and optimized automatically.
- 🧾 Legal & finance: research, summarize contracts, flag risks in seconds.
- 🛠️ Dev ops: agents run tests, deploy, and even triage failed pipelines.
- 🧑‍💼 HR & ops: onboard new hires, schedule training, and keep documentation updated.

Mini story: A small startup handed repetitive candidate-screening to an agent. Within a week, hiring velocity doubled, interviews improved, and the team refocused on culture fit — not résumé sifting. The founder called it “hiring without the busywork.”

**Bold insight: Agents won’t replace jobs — they’ll replace repetitive task layers. The real winners will be humans who design, supervise, and teach agents. If you can’t prompt, partner, or audit an agent, you’ll be managed by one.**

What repetitive task would you hand to an autonomous agent tomorrow — and why? 👇

— Jeet

Most people are watching the US–Israel–Iran tensions through the lens of military escalation and energy markets.
But there’s another layer we are not talking about enough: the disruption of the global digital workforce.
The modern digital economy is not as “borderless” as we think.
There is a clear hierarchy:
 ➡️ Projects originate in the US & Europe
 ➡️ Managed through Gulf hubs (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar)
 ➡️ Executed by talent in South Asia & Asia-Pacific
Countries like Pakistan, India, Bangladesh, and the Philippines power this system:
🇵🇰 Pakistan: ~2.3 million freelancers
🇮🇳 India: 15+ million
🇧🇩 Bangladesh:1.4 million
🇵🇭 Philippines: 1.5+ million digital workers
Now here’s the issue 👇
With rising tensions in the Middle East:
Energy prices have surged (oil > $100/barrel)
LNG disruptions are affecting Gulf economies
Business confidence is declining
And when Gulf intermediaries slow down,
 👉 Project pipelines to Asia shrink.
I’ve already started noticing signals:
→ Slower client responses
→ Fewer contracts
→ Delayed hiring decisions
This is not just market saturation.
𝐓𝐡𝐢𝐬 𝐢𝐬 𝐠𝐞𝐨𝐩𝐨𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐢𝐜𝐬 𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐭𝐡𝐞 𝐠𝐢𝐠 𝐞𝐜𝐨𝐧𝐨𝐦𝐲.
⚠️ The Bigger Risk: Cybercrime Spillover
When digital income drops, something else rises. 
History shows:
 👉 Economic disruption → Digital fraud increases
Freelancers don’t just disappear.
Some shift toward:
- Phishing
- Scams
- Social engineering
This directly connects with what my research highlights:
Crime Pattern Theory suggests that crime increases when motivated offenders encounter suitable opportunities in the absence of strong guardianship. Cybercrime is not random; it follows 𝐨𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭𝐮𝐧𝐢𝐭𝐲, 𝐯𝐮𝐥𝐧𝐞𝐫𝐚𝐛𝐢𝐥𝐢𝐭𝐲, 𝐚𝐧𝐝 𝐩𝐫𝐞𝐬𝐬𝐮𝐫𝐞.
🧠 What This Means (𝐈𝐑 𝐏𝐞𝐫𝐬𝐩𝐞𝐜𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞)
This conflict proves one thing:
1- The digital economy is now part of global power politics.
2- Energy routes affect AI infrastructure
3- War zones impact freelance markets
4- Platforms are tied to geopolitical centers
“Remote work” is not neutral anymore.
🇵🇰 What Pakistan Should Think About:
Pakistan is one of the fastest-growing freelance economies, but it is also highly exposed.
We need to start asking:
Are we too dependent on external digital pipelines?
Where are our own platforms and ecosystems?
How do we protect our digital workforce from global shocks?

Have you noticed:
A slowdown in freelance work?
Fewer opportunities recently?

Or do you think this is just competition + AI shifts?

AI won’t fix your hiring problem.

It actually might be making it worse.

Most companies are treating AI like a solution to talent shortages.

But it isn’t.

It’s a tool being used to avoid a harder conversation...

I spend time with leaders responsible for hiring at scale and what I'm seeing consistently is this:

- They invest in AI
- They improve efficiency
- And they still can’t fill the roles that matter

Because the issue was never process, it’s supply.

Truth is, Western labour markets are shrinking.

Europe is losing tens of millions of working-age people.
The US is heading towards a multi-million-worker shortfall.

You cannot solve that with better tooling.

AI can help you do more with the people you have.
But it doesn’t create new people.

And in many cases, it increases the pressure to find the right ones.

That's where the problem currently lies. 

Here’s what actually happening inside most organisations,
that ALL leaders should be aware of: 

1️⃣ AI improves productivity, but not capacity
↳ Work gets faster
↳ The number of people available doesn’t change

2️⃣ Hiring stays local
↳ Same markets
↳ Same constraints
↳ Same talent pools

3️⃣ Critical roles remain open
↳ Not because of process
↳ Because the talent isn’t there

4️⃣ AI becomes a strategic crutch
↳ “We’re investing in automation”
↳ Instead of rethinking workforce access

5️⃣ The real constraint gets ignored
↳ Demographics, not efficiency
↳ Supply, not tooling

6️⃣ Pressure builds in the system
↳ Teams stretch
↳ Delivery slows
↳ Costs rise

7️⃣ Eventually, the model gets questioned
↳ Too late
↳ Under pressure

The talent shortage isn’t global.
It’s local.

While Western markets contract, other regions are expanding rapidly.

Africa alone is adding hundreds of millions of working-age people over the coming decades.

The capability is there.
The access isn’t.

Most companies aren’t short of talent, but they are short of reach.

AI will absolutely change how work gets done.
But it won’t fix where your workforce comes from.

That requires a different decision.

The companies that solve this won’t be the ones with the best tools.

They’ll be the ones willing to move beyond the markets they’ve always hired from.

We’re putting a full report together on this, breaking down what this shift actually means for hiring strategy.

If this is something you’re dealing with, it’s worth reading, so keep an eye out!

Back on National TV in Norway. Discussing AI & Digital Twins. And the Story of world's most followed TikTok influencer, Khaby Lame, who sold his digital twin for 1bn USD // Hyggelig start på morgenen hos God Morgen Norge på TV 2 hvor jeg snakker om AI og Digital Tvillinger🇳🇴 

I was invited back  on national TV (God Morgen Norge) as their Tech & AI expert to unpack the Khaby Lame deal for a national audience - and the deeper I went, the more I realized we're looking at a genuinely new category of business that most people are still filing away as celebrity gossip.

Deal summarized; The world's most-followed TikToker signed a deal letting a company build an AI-powered version of him - his face, voice, and mannerisms - that can work on his behalf for the next 36 months in a deal worth 1bn USD to a Hong Kong based company. Not for cash. For 75 million shares in stock that has since crashed over 90%.

For most of modern business history, the scarcest thing a person had was their time. You sold hours. You sold presence. You sold being in the room. Khaby did something different. He didn't sell his time - he licensed the pattern of himself, so an AI twin can show up in rooms he'll never walk into. Mandarin in Shanghai at 3 AM. Norwegian in Oslo at breakfast. Simultaneously.

That's not an endorsement deal. That's infrastructure.

There's also a massive opportunity here that people are overlooking. Forget celebrities for a moment. Imagine a small company hiring a multilingual AI host to sell their products in Asia overnight. No export team. No travel budget. No time zones. That's a real unlock for businesses everywhere. As an investor, I see the upside clearly.

But I also know what happens when new infrastructure arrives. When the tractor came to farming, the horse lost its job. We need to make sure photographers, voice actors, presenters, and creative professionals don't become the horse in this story.

And there are some key ethical questions; What if you regret it? What if your digital version promotes something you'd never endorse? What if the company that owns your twin outlasts your contract - or your interest in the deal?

Because if the digital version of us can keep working forever - as digital ghosts - we have to decide now, while the frameworks are still soft, whether identity is just another asset class, or whether there's something about being a person that should remain non-transferable.

I don't have all the answers. Nobody does yet. But part of my role - whether on national television, in boardrooms, or with the founders I work with - is to help people see around the corner. Not to predict the future, but to ask the right questions before it arrives.

Grateful to TV 2 and God Morgen Norge for continuing to invite me into these conversations. 🎙

What's your views on this topic? Would you want your own digital twin?

The UAE Job Market Paradox of 2026: High Optimism, Rising Competition & More Selective Hiring — How to Actually Land the Role

76% of UAE employees say it’s a great time to find a job — one of the highest figures globally.  

Yet, competition is fiercer than ever.  
Employers are more selective.  
And many talented professionals (especially expats) are hearing “not enough UAE experience” — even for mid-level roles.

This is the real paradox of the UAE job market right now:  
Strong hiring momentum + 500,000+ annual openings, but narrower filters, skills-first evaluation, and intense competition from global talent flooding in.

What’s changed?

- Skills-based hiring is no longer optional — Degrees matter less. What you can actually do (AI tools, data literacy, cybersecurity awareness, project delivery, cross-cultural communication) matters more.
- UAE/local market experience still carries huge weight for many recruiters — especially in construction, real estate, finance, and operations.
- Networking and digital presence have become non-negotiable. A strong LinkedIn profile with tangible achievements often beats a perfect CV.
- Project-based and contract roles are rising as companies test talent before making permanent offers.

The good news? Those who adapt are moving faster than ever.

Practical tips to stand out in 2026:

1. Build proof of skills — Certifications, portfolio projects, or measurable results in AI, cloud, data, or domain-specific tools beat generic experience.
2. Bridge the UAE experience gap — Highlight transferable skills from similar markets, do short-term gigs/freelance projects in the region, or network aggressively via industry events and LinkedIn.
3. Optimize your digital profile — Update your LinkedIn headline and summary with keywords recruiters actually search for. Show impact, not just responsibilities.
4. Leverage referrals and relationships — In a selective market, who you know (or who knows you) can open doors faster than cold applications.
5. Consider contract/project roles — They’re a smart entry point to gain UAE exposure and prove your value.

The UAE job market rewards those who are proactive, adaptable, and visible.

What’s your biggest challenge in the UAE job hunt right now?  
→ Lack of local experience?  
→ Standing out in a sea of applications?  
→ Navigating skills-based interviews?  

Drop your thoughts below 👇 I’d love to hear (and maybe share some targeted advice in the comments).

We're hiring: Technical Automation Specialist (HubSpot + n8n)
Remote | 25+ hrs/week | Long-term
We're an AI automation consulting firm based in Austria & Poland. We restructure how businesses operate — CRM architecture, workflow automation, system migrations. Not theory. Real production systems for real companies.
We need someone who has actually built things in HubSpot and n8n. Not watched tutorials. Built them. Broken them. Fixed them at 11pm because a client's Zapier just died.

→ You can self-host n8n on a VPS and it doesn't scare you

25+ hours/week. Starts as contract with a path to full-time for the right person.
Interested? Send your CV and a short note about a system you've built or fixed to: aishasarwar38@gmail.com
No agencies. No "I'm a fast learner." Show us what you've done.
